summary:
The Hanging Gardens of Babylon — one of the Seven Wonders of the ancient world — are the only Wonder whose existence is archaeologically uncertain. Classical and Hellenistic authors (including Diodorus Siculus, Strabo, and Quintus Curtius) described them as a series of tiered terraces planted with trees, shrubs, and flowers, rising like a mountain above the flat Babylonian plain, visible from a great distance, and irrigated by mechanical devices raising water from the Euphrates. Tradition attributed them to Nebuchadnezzar II, who reportedly built them for his Median wife Amytis.

Despite extensive excavations at Babylon, archaeologists have found no conclusive evidence of the Gardens — no drainage systems, no unusual terraced structures, and Babylonian records mentioning many other building projects make no reference to them. This has led scholar Stephanie Dalley to propose that the Hanging Gardens were actually built at Nineveh by the Assyrian king Sennacherib, and that later Greek sources transferred the story to the more famous Babylon. Sennacherib's own inscriptions describe an elaborate garden watered by an aqueduct — and the physical evidence at Nineveh is more consistent with such a structure. Whether Babylon or Nineveh, the Gardens represent the ancient world's concept of the ultimate luxury: a green paradise of cultivated nature constructed at enormous effort in an arid landscape.
